method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island was once known as the Isle of Sand, reflecting its low, flat and sandy character.[1][4] Its present name honours Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette ''Dauphine''.[1] Tromelin reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had survived there for 15 years after being stranded.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a low-lying, flat coral island of about 1 square kilometre in the Indian Ocean, approximately 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[4] It is administered by France as part of the Scattered Islands district of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands.[4] The island is uninhabited apart from a meteorological station, in operation since 1949, which monitors cyclones and supports regional weather forecasting.[4] It has sparse vegetation, described as consisting of a few low bushes and no trees.[5]

The island’s sandy coral surface is influenced by wind-driven sediment movement, and studies of Indian Ocean coral islands identify Tromelin as a place where aeolian sedimentation contributes to island topography.[3] Wind speeds above 8 metres per second on Tromelin are associated with a narrow directional range between 100° and 140°.[3] Tromelin also functions as a sea turtle sanctuary, with conservation efforts directed at protecting nesting turtles from invasive species and habitat threats.[4][5]

Before the introduction of rats, Tromelin supported a variety of seabirds and native plants, but invasive rodents severely damaged the ecosystem.[1] By the time eradication efforts were undertaken, only a few hundred breeding pairs of masked boobies and red-footed boobies remained among the island’s seabirds.[2] French authorities removed the rats in 2005 using poisoned bait, completing the eradication in about a month.[2] Following the removal of rats, native plants began to recover and three bird species returned to the island without additional seabird-attraction measures such as decoys or recorded calls.[1][2] Tromelin is now regarded as an important reference ecosystem for environmental research and an example of island recovery after invasive mammal eradication.[1]

==Fauna and flora==
The island’s native land fauna is naturally limited, with seabirds forming the dominant group and nesting green sea turtles (''Chelonia mydas'') the principal non-avian vertebrate; no native terrestrial mammals, reptiles or amphibians are known.[1] Before rat predation altered the ecosystem, Tromelin supported six to eight seabird species in large numbers, but by 2005 only two booby species were still breeding, and with limited success.[1] Following rat eradication, the breeding seabird community increased to seven species by 2022, while the number of breeding pairs rose from 353 to 4,758.[1] Surveys from 2017 to 2021 also recorded eleven non-breeding seabird species, including frigatebirds that used the island as a regular roost and tropicbirds whose behaviour suggested possible future colonisation.[3] The recovery has been described as a notable case of seabird recolonisation after invasive-mammal removal, although mice remained the only introduced mammals reported in the later study period.[3][4]

The native flora consists mainly of hardy littoral plants adapted to wind, salt spray and scarce freshwater, historically centred on the shrub ''Tournefortia argentea'' (syn. ''Heliotropium foertherianum'') together with fewer than ten herbaceous species.[1] No native trees are recorded, although introduced coconut palms (''Cocos nucifera'') occur locally without dominating the plant assemblage.[1] Rat impacts reduced native plant cover by consuming seeds, seedlings and foliage, but monitoring after eradication recorded recovery to seven native taxa by 2013, including increased density and height of ''Tournefortia argentea''.[1] Vegetation recovery has also been considered ecologically important for seabirds, by improving nesting habitat and contributing to wider nutrient flows from the restored seabird population.[1][4]

==History==
The island was first recorded by the French navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée in the 1720s, who named it Île de Sable, meaning “Isle of Sand”.[1]

In July 1761, the French ship Utile was wrecked near Tromelin while carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people.[2] About twenty sailors and at least seventy enslaved people died, many of the latter trapped below deck after the hatches had been locked or nailed down.[2] The surviving castaways remained on the island for fifteen years, and when rescue came in 1776 only eight people were found alive: seven women and a boy born during the ordeal.[2][3] They were rescued by Captain Chevalier de Tromelin aboard Dauphine, taken to Mauritius and later freed.[1][3] The island was subsequently named in honour of Tromelin.[3]

Later accounts by British sailors recorded remnants of stone houses and carefully arranged graves on the island.[2] Archaeological and diving investigations sponsored by UNESCO later documented walls of dwellings built from coral blocks and compacted sand, as well as a large oven containing remains of turtles, birds and shellfish.[2] The wreck of the Utile and the survival of the castaways have since become an important episode in the historical memory of slavery and survival in the Indian Ocean.[2][5]

France bases its sovereignty claim on the arrival of Dauphine on 29 November 1776, while Mauritius argues that Tromelin should have passed to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and should not have remained attached to Réunion.[1] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over the island.[1]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate (Köppen Aw), with consistently warm temperatures and a distinct wet season from November to April influenced by the intertropical convergence zone.[2] The mean annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, with little seasonal variation because of the island’s tropical oceanic setting.[2] Annual precipitation is approximately 1,074 mm, concentrated in the wetter summer months; February is among the wettest months, while May to October is comparatively dry.[2] Prevailing southeast trade winds are frequent and often strong, contributing to high evaporation and persistent breezy conditions.[2] The island’s isolated position in the western Indian Ocean makes it useful for monitoring tropical cyclones affecting the region, particularly systems moving toward Madagascar.[4] A weather station has operated on the island since the mid-20th century and continues to support regional weather observation and forecasting.[2][4]
